wa-upg.3.7: timing determinism guidelines for contributors

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-upg.1.4","title":"Human command: wa reproduce replay (deterministic incident replayer)","description":"# Task: `wa reproduce replay`

## Goal
Make incident bundles actionable by providing deterministic replay tooling that reproduces key outcomes from the bundle.

## Requirements
- Command: `wa reproduce replay <bundle>`
- Supported replay modes (initial):
  - `policy`: re-run PolicyEngine evaluation on recorded decision context and assert the same result
  - `rules`: re-run rule/pattern engine on recorded segments and assert expected detections
- Output:
  - TTY: readable summary of pass/fail with pointers to traces
  - `--format json`: structured results (per-check) with stable error codes
- Failure handling:
  - if replay cannot be performed (missing data), print a clear explanation and "what to do next".

## Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - bundle load + manifest validation
  - replay determinism on fixtures
- Integration tests:
  - golden bundles in `tests/fixtures/incident_bundles/*`

## Acceptance Criteria
- A valid bundle can be replayed and yields deterministic outputs.
- Replay failures are actionable and do not leak secrets.

 {"id":"wa-upg.1.5","title":"Tests: incident bundle + replay (unit/integration fixtures)","description":"# Task: Tests for incident bundle + replay

## Goal
Make incident bundling/replay trustworthy by adding comprehensive unit + integration tests.

## Requirements
- Add deterministic fixtures:
  - fixture DB with a small set of segments/events
  - fixture logs with known secret tokens to verify redaction
  - fixture decision trace(s)
- Test categories:
  - bundle building (layout + manifest)
  - privacy budget enforcement
  - redaction correctness (no secrets anywhere)
  - replay correctness (policy + rules modes)

## Logging/artifacts
- On test failure, print paths to the generated bundle directory and minimal diff hints.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests cover the main success paths and the important failure modes (missing data, invalid schema, over-budget truncation).
- Failures are actionable and do not require manual digging.

 {"id":"wa-upg.1.6","title":"E2E: incident bundle export + replay (verbose logs + artifacts)","description":"# Task: E2E incident bundle export + replay

## Goal
Add an end-to-end scenario that proves incident bundle export and replay works against a real-ish runtime.

## Scenario
- Start a watcher in a controlled test workspace.
- Produce a deterministic incident:
  - example: trigger a known policy denial (AltScreen or recent GAP) OR a known rule match.
- Run `wa reproduce` to export a bundle.
- Run `wa reproduce replay` and assert the expected replay result.

## Requirements
- No fixed sleeps. Use wait-for/quiescence utilities with bounded timeouts.
- Verbose logs:
  - command transcript
  - watcher logs (captured)
  - paths to artifacts
- Artifacts:
  - the generated bundle
  - a small "summary.json" with key assertions

## Acceptance Criteria
- E2E is deterministic and debuggable.
- On failure, artifacts are sufficient to reproduce locally.

-{"id":"wa-upg.1.7","title":"Docs: incident bundles + wa reproduce (shareable support workflow)","description":"# Task: Document incident bundles and `wa reproduce`

## Goal
Make incident bundles discoverable and usable by humans and by support processes.

## Requirements
- Document:
  - when to run `wa reproduce`
  - what data is included by default (and what is not)
  - privacy implications + opt-in flags
  - how to replay a bundle
  - how to attach to a GitHub issue (or share internally)
- Include examples of:
  - a policy denial bundle
  - a rule replay bundle
  - a watcher crash bundle

## Acceptance Criteria
- A new contributor can follow docs to generate and replay a bundle without guesswork.

 {"id":"wa-upg.10","title":"[EPIC] Schema-driven docs + client generation (robot/MCP as real API)","description":"# [EPIC] Schema-driven docs + client generation

## Mission
Treat wa's robot/MCP interfaces as a real API:
- schemas are authoritative
- docs are generated from schemas
- typed clients can be generated or maintained with confidence

## Why This Matters
If an integration breaks silently, users lose trust.
Schemas + generated docs/types provide:
- contract stability
- faster integration development
- safer refactors

## Scope
- Build on the existing JSON Schema work:
  - versioned schemas for robot outputs and error objects
- Add:
  - schema-driven docs pages (render schemas into readable reference)
  - optional client generation (at least one language) OR type-safe Rust client crate
  - CI checks to ensure schema/doc generation stays in sync

## Success Criteria
- Another tool can generate types and call wa robot/MCP reliably.
- Schema changes are intentional and reviewed.

## Testing
- Schema validations in CI.
- Golden tests for generated docs output stability.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Docs reflect schemas automatically.
- At least one typed client path exists and is tested.

 {"id":"wa-upg.10.1","title":"Design: schema→docs/types strategy (direction, versioning, languages)","description":"# Task: Decide schema-driven generation strategy

## Goal
Choose the practical strategy for schema-driven docs and typed clients.

## Requirements
- Decide generation direction:
  - Rust structs → JSON Schema (preferred for single source of truth)
  - OR JSON Schema → types (and keep schemas authored by hand)
- Decide client target(s):
  - TypeScript (common for tooling)
  - Python (common for scripts)
  - Rust client crate (for in-repo safety)
- Decide versioning policy:
  - how schema versions map to wa versions
  - how breaking changes are detected

## Acceptance Criteria
- Strategy is chosen and consistent with repo goals (robust, low tech debt).
","notes":"Decision documented in PLAN.md section 21.5; ready to close once parent unblocked.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"GrayRidge","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T20:48:47.647300556Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-07T00:19:50.945432357Z","closed_at":"2026-02-07T00:19:50.945306944Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-design","area-docs","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-upg.10.1","depends_on_id":"wa-upg.10","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:44Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":226,"issue_id":"wa-upg.10.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Strategy decided: Rust structs → JSON Schema via schemars (single source of truth). Client priority: Rust P0, TypeScript P1, Python P2. Versioning: schema tracks wa semver, golden-file CI test for drift. Implemented ApiVersion (pre/post-1.0 compat), SchemaRegistry (24 endpoints), ChangeKind classification, SchemaDiffResult. 27 tests. Commit f15fcb4.","created_at":"2026-02-06T18:53:37Z"}]}
 {"id":"wa-upg.10.2","title":"Implement schema-driven docs generator (reference pages)","description":"# Task: Implement schema-driven docs generator

## Goal
Generate readable docs pages from the canonical schemas.

## Requirements
- Generate docs that include:
  - command list
  - request/response objects
  - error objects and codes
  - examples
- Output stability:
  - deterministic ordering
  - stable formatting (golden tests)

## Testing
- Golden tests for generated docs.
- CI check that generated output matches committed files.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Docs pages are usable as an API reference without reading code.

 {"id":"wa-upg.3.4","title":"Refactor E2E scripts: remove sleeps; use wait-for/quiescence helpers","description":"# Task: Refactor E2E scripts to avoid fixed sleeps

## Goal
Systematically remove fixed sleeps from E2E scripts and replace them with deterministic wait-for/quiescence.

## Requirements
- Audit `scripts/e2e_test.sh` cases and identify any usage of:
  - `sleep`
  - implicit timing assumptions ("wait 1s")
- Replace with:
  - wait-for pane text / marker
  - wait-for watcher health signal
  - wait-for quiescence
- Improve logs to show:
  - condition waited for
  - elapsed time
  - last observed state

## Acceptance Criteria
- E2E scripts have no fixed sleeps except for extremely rare cases (must be justified in the script).
- Timing failures provide enough context to diagnose.

 {"id":"wa-upg.3.5","title":"Tests: wait-for/quiescence helpers (unit+integration)","description":"# Task: Tests for wait-for/quiescence helpers

## Goal
Prevent regressions in the timing primitives.

## Requirements
- Unit tests for:
  - backoff schedule behavior
  - timeout error contents
- Integration tests for:
  - quiescence with synthetic producer/consumer

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests fail with actionable output when timing primitives regress.

 {"id":"wa-upg.3.6","title":"E2E: flake guard mode (repeat-run a representative case)","description":"# Task: E2E flake guard mode

## Goal
Add an optional CI mode (or local mode) that reruns one representative E2E case multiple times to detect timing flakiness early.

## Requirements
- Implement a runner flag (or separate script mode) to run a chosen case N times.
- Ensure artifacts are retained on first failure with:
  - per-iteration logs
  - summary of timings

## Acceptance Criteria
- Flake guard mode can catch timing regressions without making normal CI too slow.

 {"id":"wa-upg.4","title":"[EPIC] Stable pane identity handshake (pane_uuid across churn)","description":"# [EPIC] Stable pane identity handshake (`pane_uuid`)

## Mission
Give every pane a **stable identity** (`pane_uuid`) that survives common churn:
- renames
- title changes
- tab reparenting / workspace moves
- session recording and replay
- (eventually) distributed mode

This unlocks higher-trust automation:
- fewer "sent to wrong pane" failures
- better correlation across timelines
- durable per-pane histories

## Why This Matters
WezTerm `pane_id` is useful but not sufficient as a long-lived identity:
- IDs can be reused across sessions
- names and titles change
- in distributed mode we need a stable cross-machine identifier

A stable `pane_uuid` lets the system reason about "this is the same pane" even when superficial attributes change.

## Design Constraints
- Observation loop should be **pure read** when possible.
- If we must write to establish identity, it must be:
  - explicit, minimal, and audited
  - safe and reversible
  - never continuous spam

## Candidate Approaches (evaluate)
1) WezTerm user vars (preferred if list/get-text surfaces them)
2) OSC marker handshake (write-once marker captured by watcher)
3) Pure fingerprint (fallback): derive a probabilistic identity from stable features (domain + cwd + creation time)

## Success Criteria
- For a pane that is renamed and moved, wa continues to associate output/events with the same stable identity.
- Identity assignment is safe-by-default and does not violate observe/act separation without explicit justification.

## Testing
- Unit tests for identity parsing/derivation.
- Integration tests with synthetic pane registry updates.
- E2E:
  - create pane, assign `pane_uuid`, rename/move, verify `pane_uuid` stable

## Acceptance Criteria
- A clear identity strategy is selected and implemented.
- `pane_uuid` is stored in DB and exposed via robot/CLI.
- Tests and at least one E2E scenario validate stability.
","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T20:39:27.015654760Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T20:39:27.015654760Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-ingest","area-storage","area-tests","area-wezterm","phase-1"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-upg.4","depends_on_id":"wa-upg","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:47Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-upg.4.1","title":"Research: best pane_uuid strategy with WezTerm (user vars/OSC/fingerprint)","description":"# Task: Research pane_uuid strategy

## Goal
Determine the best, safest mechanism to assign and read a stable `pane_uuid`.

## Questions to answer
- Does `wezterm cli list --format json` expose user vars or any stable per-pane metadata?
- Can wa set a per-pane user var without sending visible text?
- Can we use an OSC sequence that:
  - is not visible to the user
  - can be observed by the watcher
  - is safe and idempotent
- If none of the above, what is the best fingerprint fallback and what are the collision risks?

## Deliverable
- A written decision (in this bead) selecting:
  - primary approach
  - fallback approach
  - any required opt-in flags

## Acceptance Criteria
- The chosen approach is feasible with WezTerm primitives and aligns with observe/act safety constraints.
","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T20:39:40.327746299Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-22T04:31:44.118227734Z","closed_at":"2026-01-22T04:31:44.118112177Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-design","area-wezterm","phase-1"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-upg.4.1","depends_on_id":"wa-upg.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:47Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-upg.4.2","title":"Spec: pane_uuid lifecycle, storage schema, and invariants","description":"# Task: Spec pane_uuid lifecycle + schema

## Goal
Specify how `pane_uuid` is created, stored, and used across the system.

## Requirements
- Lifecycle:
  - when assigned
  - when re-assigned (if ever)
  - how to handle panes that cannot be assigned (fallback)
- Storage:
  - store `pane_uuid` in panes table
  - store historical mapping of `pane_id` → `pane_uuid` (optional) for auditing
- Invariants:
  - within a workspace, `pane_uuid` is unique
  - identity assignment is idempotent
  - no silent reassignment that could lead to "wrong pane" actions
- Exposure:
  - robot state includes `pane_uuid`
  - CLI shows `pane_uuid` in verbose modes

## Testing
- Define fixture expectations for:
  - rename
  - move
  - pane disappearance and reappearance

## Acceptance Criteria
- Spec clearly defines success/failure behavior and the safety posture.
","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T20:39:51.568740781Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-22T04:31:46.393291299Z","closed_at":"2026-01-22T04:31:46.393198495Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-design","area-storage","area-wezterm","phase-1"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-upg.4.2","depends_on_id":"wa-upg.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:47Z","created_by":"import"}]}

@@ -0,0 +1,317 @@
+# Timing Determinism Guidelines
+
+wa uses condition-based waiting throughout. Every wait is driven by an
+observable state change — a pattern match, a queue drain, or an external
+signal — rather than a fixed delay. This document explains the available
+patterns, when to use each, and how to write deterministic tests.
+
+## The No-Sleep Rule
+
+**Do not use `std::thread::sleep()` or `tokio::time::sleep()` with a
+fixed duration in production code.** Fixed sleeps are probabilistic: they
+either wait too long (slow) or not long enough (flaky).
+
+The only sanctioned exception is the chaos module (`chaos.rs`), which
+uses blocking sleep to simulate slow I/O as a deliberate fault injection.
+
+If you find yourself reaching for `sleep(Duration::from_secs(2))`, use
+one of the patterns below instead.
+
+## Three Levels of Waiting
+
+wa provides waiting primitives at three granularities. Choose the
+narrowest one that fits your situation.
+
+### Level 1: Generic `wait_for` with Backoff
+
+For waiting on an arbitrary condition:
+
+```rust
+use wa_core::wait::{wait_for, wait_for_condition, Backoff};
+use std::time::Duration;
+
+// Simple boolean condition
+let result = wait_for_condition(
+    || async { check_something().await },
+    Duration::from_secs(10),
+).await?;
+
+// With custom backoff
+let result = wait_for_condition_with_backoff(
+    || async { check_something().await },
+    Duration::from_secs(10),
+    Backoff {
+        initial: Duration::from_millis(50),
+        max: Duration::from_secs(2),
+        factor: 2,
+        max_retries: Some(20),
+    },
+).await?;
+```
+
+The `Backoff` struct controls polling intervals:
+
+| Field | Default | Purpose |
+|-------|---------|---------|
+| `initial` | 25 ms | First poll interval |
+| `max` | 1 s | Upper bound on interval |
+| `factor` | 2 | Multiplier per retry |
+| `max_retries` | None | Optional retry cap |
+
+Backoff grows exponentially: 25 ms → 50 ms → 100 ms → ... → 1 s (cap).
+The final poll is clamped to the remaining timeout so the total wait
+never exceeds the deadline.
+
+### Level 2: Pane Pattern Matching (`PaneWaiter`)
+
+For waiting until a pane's output matches a pattern:
+
+```rust
+use wa_core::wezterm::{PaneWaiter, WaitMatcher, WaitOptions};
+
+let waiter = PaneWaiter::new(source, WaitOptions::default());
+let result = waiter.wait_for(
+    pane_id,
+    &WaitMatcher::substring("$ "),  // wait for shell prompt
+    Duration::from_secs(30),
+).await?;
+
+match result {
+    WaitResult::Matched { elapsed_ms, polls } => { /* success */ }
+    WaitResult::TimedOut { elapsed_ms, polls, .. } => { /* handle timeout */ }
+}
+```
+
+`WaitOptions` controls the polling behavior:
+
+| Field | Default | Purpose |
+|-------|---------|---------|
+| `tail_lines` | 200 | Lines of output to check |
+| `escapes` | false | Include ANSI escape sequences |
+| `poll_initial` | 50 ms | Initial poll interval |
+| `poll_max` | 1 s | Maximum poll interval |
+| `max_polls` | 10,000 | Safety limit |
+
+The CLI equivalent is `wa send --wait-for`:
+
+```bash
+wa send 3 "ls -la" --wait-for "\\$" --timeout-secs 30
+wa send 3 "grep error" --wait-for-regex "found \\d+ matches"
+```
+
+Use `WaitMatcher::substring()` for simple prompts and
+`WaitMatcher::regex()` for structured output.
+
+### Level 3: System Quiescence
+
+For waiting until all subsystems are idle (queues drained, no recent
+activity):
+
+```rust
+use wa_core::wait::{
+    QuiescenceDetector, QueueDepthGauge, ActivityTracker,
+    wait_for_quiescence,
+};
+
+// Set up gauges for each queue
+let ingest_gauge = Arc::new(QueueDepthGauge::new("ingest"));
+let storage_gauge = Arc::new(QueueDepthGauge::new("storage"));
+let activity = Arc::new(ActivityTracker::new());
+
+let detector = QuiescenceDetector::new(Duration::from_millis(500))
+    .with_gauge(ingest_gauge.clone())
+    .with_gauge(storage_gauge.clone())
+    .with_activity(activity.clone());
+
+// In production code: increment/decrement gauges around work
+ingest_gauge.increment();
+// ... do work ...
+ingest_gauge.decrement();
+activity.record();
+
+// Wait for all queues to drain and no activity for 500ms
+wait_for_quiescence(detector, Duration::from_secs(10)).await?;
+```
+
+The quiescence detector considers the system quiet when:
+1. All `QueueDepthGauge` totals are zero (no pending work)
+2. `ActivityTracker` has not been touched for the configured quiet window
+
+Use this for shutdown sequences, test teardown, and any situation where
+you need all asynchronous pipelines to finish.
+
+## Adaptive Polling in the Tailer
+
+The per-pane tailer uses adaptive polling to balance responsiveness and
+CPU usage:
+
+| State | Interval | Behavior |
+|-------|----------|----------|
+| Active (changes detected) | 50 ms | Reset to fast polling |
+| Idle (no changes) | 50 ms → 75 ms → 112 ms → ... → 1 s | Exponential backoff (1.5x) |
+| Backpressure (5+ send failures) | Paused | Insert explicit GAP, reset |
+
+Configuration lives in `TailerConfig`:
+
+```rust
+TailerConfig {
+    min_interval: Duration::from_millis(50),
+    max_interval: Duration::from_secs(1),
+    backoff_multiplier: 1.5,
+    max_concurrent: 10,
+    overlap_size: 256,
+    send_timeout: Duration::from_millis(100),
+}
+```
+
+When a pane produces output, its tailer immediately drops back to
+`min_interval`. When idle, it gradually slows to `max_interval`. This
+avoids wasting CPU on quiet panes while responding quickly to active ones.
+
+## Explicit GAP Semantics
+
+When wa cannot guarantee continuity — overlap matching fails, alt-screen
+blocks stable capture, or backpressure overflows — it records an explicit
+**GAP segment** and emits a gap event.
+
+GAPs are treated as uncertainty:
+- Policy checks can require approval when recent gaps are present
+- Search results flag text near gaps as potentially incomplete
+- Diagnostics surface gap counts and causes
+
+The design principle: **no data loss goes unrecorded.** A GAP is better
+than silently dropping data or pretending continuity exists.
+
+## Timeout Handling
+
+All timeouts follow the same structure:
+
+1. **Wall-clock deadline:** `start + timeout`
+2. **Remaining time clamping:** Sleep duration never exceeds remaining
+   time
+3. **Max retry limit:** Optional `max_retries` stops polling early
+4. **Safety limit:** `max_polls` cap prevents runaway loops
+
+When a timeout fires, the error includes diagnostic context:
+
+```rust
+WaitError {
+    expected: "prompt marker '$ '",
+    last_observed: Some("... building project ..."),
+    retries: 47,
+    elapsed: Duration::from_secs(30),
+}
+
+// Display: "timeout waiting for prompt marker '$ ' after 30000ms
+//          (retries=47, last_observed='... building project ...')"
+```
+
+This makes timeout failures actionable: the caller knows what was
+expected, what was last seen, and how many attempts were made.
+
+## Retry Policies
+
+For external calls (WezTerm CLI, database writes), use `RetryPolicy`:
+
+```rust
+use wa_core::retry::RetryPolicy;
+
+// Pre-configured policies
+let wezterm = RetryPolicy::wezterm_cli();  // 3 attempts, 100ms initial
+let db = RetryPolicy::db_write();          // 5 attempts, 50ms initial
+```
+
+Retry policies add jitter (±10% by default) to prevent thundering herd
+on shared resources. They integrate with the circuit breaker for
+connection-level fault tolerance.
+
+## Writing Deterministic Tests
+
+### Unit tests
+
+Use `tokio::time::pause()` to control time in async tests:
+
+```rust
+#[tokio::test]
+async fn test_wait_for_timeout() {
+    tokio::time::pause();
+
+    let result = wait_for_condition(
+        || async { false },  // never succeeds
+        Duration::from_secs(5),
+    ).await;
+
+    assert!(result.is_err());
+    // Test completes instantly — no real wall-clock wait
+}
+```
+
+For synchronous timing logic, inject a clock trait or use
+`std::time::Instant` comparisons rather than `sleep()`.
+
+### Integration tests
+
+Use pattern-based waits, not fixed delays:
+
+```rust
+// BAD: flaky, slow, non-deterministic
+tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_secs(5)).await;
+let text = get_pane_text(pane_id).await;
+assert!(text.contains("ready"));
+
+// GOOD: deterministic, fast, self-documenting
+let result = waiter.wait_for(
+    pane_id,
+    &WaitMatcher::substring("ready"),
+    Duration::from_secs(30),
+).await?;
+assert!(matches!(result, WaitResult::Matched { .. }));
+```
+
+### E2E tests
+
+The E2E harness (`scripts/e2e_test.sh`) enforces timing discipline:
+
+- **Global timeout:** Each scenario has a configurable timeout (default
+  120s, exit code 4 on breach)
+- **Artifact collection:** `duration_ms` is recorded per scenario for
+  performance tracking
+- **No real AI:** Scenarios use scripted output so results are
+  deterministic
+- **Pattern detection:** All waits use `wa robot wait-for` or equivalent,
+  not `sleep`
+
+```bash
+# Run with custom timeout
+./scripts/e2e_test.sh --timeout 60 my_scenario
+
+# Verbose output includes timing
+./scripts/e2e_test.sh --verbose --keep-artifacts my_scenario
+```
+
+## Contributor Checklist
+
+Before submitting code that involves waiting or timing:
+
+- [ ] No `std::thread::sleep()` in production code
+- [ ] No `tokio::time::sleep()` with a fixed duration in production code
+- [ ] Waits use `wait_for`, `PaneWaiter`, or `QuiescenceDetector`
+- [ ] Timeouts include `WaitError` with `expected` and `last_observed`
+- [ ] Backoff intervals start small (25–50 ms) and cap at 1–2 s
+- [ ] Tests use `tokio::time::pause()` or pattern matching, not real
+  delays
+- [ ] Continuity gaps are recorded explicitly (GAP segments)
+- [ ] Retry policies use jitter to prevent thundering herd
+
+### Exception process
+
+If you genuinely need a fixed delay (hardware settling, rate limit
+cooldown with no signal), document the reason in a comment:
+
+```rust
+// Fixed delay: rate-limit API requires minimum 1s between calls
+// and provides no retry-after header. See WA-4002.
+tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_secs(1)).await;
+```
+
+This makes the exception visible and grep-able for future cleanup.
